content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 80 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration           Executive leg               Enforcement             Conflict of Interest

916 322 5660                    322 5921                    322 6441                    322 6444


November 17, 1977


Mr. John Hodgson
Dobbs and Nielsen
1127 Eleventh Street
Suite 544
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Hodgson:

     Thank you for your letter of November 14, requesting staff
advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act. The facts, as I understand them,
are as follows.

     Dudley Maxwell is a city councilman in the City of Fortuna.
He previously owned two pieces of property which are currently
owned by Mr. Jack Braun. Mr. Braun is a source of income to Mr.
Maxwell pursuant to Government Code §87103(c) because he has
made payments to Mr. Maxwell relating to the sale of the property over the past 12 months. One of the parcels owned by Mr.
Braun is the subject of a proposed variance to the City's
General Plan. You have asked whether Mr. Maxwell can vote
on the proposed variance if, prior to the vote, Mr. Braun
sells both parcels to L&H, the developer of the contemplated
project.

     The determinative question is whether Mr. Maxwell's vote
or participation in the decision to amend the General Plan will
have a material financial effect on Mr. Braun, who is a source
of income to Mr. Maxwell. In light of the facts set forth
above, we conclude that Mr. Maxwell's vote will have a material
financial effect on Mr. Braun if Mr. Braun sells his property
to the developer and any of the following circumstances exist.

     1. The contract of sale is in any way contingent upon
        the outcome of any vote by a public body;

     2. Mr. Braun retains any equitable interest in the property, any right to future consideration, any right or
        obligation to repurchase the property, or has any
        understanding or expectation of future payments,

John Hodgson
November 17, 1977
Page Two


business or leaseback arrangements from the purchaser
in connection with the sale of the property;

3.  The sales price reflects an appreciation in the value
of the property due to the anticipated amendment of
the General Plan concerning the proposed shopping
center project in question.*


Obviously, any contract of sale that was based on the
contingencies, rights, understandings or expectations described
in the first two items above would be materially affected by
the vote in question. The third item, on the other hand, can-
not be applied as a general rule to all contracts of sale which
are entered into by a person who is a source of income to an
official. However, because of the personal relationship existing
between Mr. Maxwell and Mr. Braun and the circumstances
under which the sale arose, we think it is an accurate test
of materiality in the instant case.

We are advised that Mr. Maxwell has informed the parties
involved that he intends to vote for the proposed amendment to
the General Plan. It is also our understanding that Mr. Braun
is Mr. Maxwell's attorney and that they have a long-standing
personal and business relationship. Moreover, the decision of
whether to amend the General Plan will have a material effect
on at least one, and probably both, of the parcels owned by
Mr. Braun. Accordingly, there is every reason to believe
that in negotiating with the developer for the sale of these
parcels, Mr. Braun will be able to act with confidence that
Mr. Maxwell will vote in favor of the proposed amendment to
the General Plan if he is not disqualified.

It is also important to note that Mr. Braun already has
taken actions in an attempt to free Mr. Maxwell from the
disqualification provisions of the Act. Acting on an anti-
apprehension as to the effect of §87103(c), Mr. Braun paid,
in full, the note owing to Mr. Maxwell arising from the previous purchase of one of the two parcels in question. Mr.
Braun now proposes to sell both of his parcels to the developer in order to free Mr. Maxwell from the disqualification
provisions of the Act.

*The cited possible appreciation in value of the property
in question would be calculated based on the difference, if any,
between the fair market value of the property prior to the
institution of efforts to re-zone the property in connection
with the project and the fair market value of the property
assuming such re-zoning was in fact accomplished.

John Hodgson
November 17, 1977
Page Three

Under these unique facts, we think that Mr. Braun never
would have sold his property at this time were it not for his
reliance on Mr. Maxwell's vote. We, therefore, conclude that
the vote will have a material financial effect on Mr. Braun
if he proceeds in appreciation value from [illegible] for the project
in anticipation of a change to the City's General Plan to
allow development of the proposed shopping center.

In summary, Mr. Maxwell may vote on the proposed amendment to the General Plan if the sales agreement transferring
the parcels in question from Mr. Braun to the developer does
not reflect any of the three elements set forth above. If
you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call
me.

Sincerely,


Ted Prim
Chief, Conflicts of Interest
Division


TP:gb

cc: Dudley Maxwell
    Sam Stone
    John Gromal
    Ronald Angell

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law
SUITE 544, 1127 ELEVENTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

(916) 446-6752

November 14, 1977


Ted Prim, Esq., Chief
Conflicts Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

RE:  Opinion Request 77034

Dear Mr. Prim:

This office represents L & H International, a development
company that is interested in building a shopping center complex in the City of Fortuna.

We are aware that Mr. Dudley Maxwell, one of the City Council
members, has been concerned about a possible conflict of
interest because he  formerly owned 40 acres of land, including
acreage upon which L & H has an option to purchase for its
planned project.

L & H believes that the proposed development would be good for
the City of Fortuna.  L & H is also aware that California Government Code §87100 prohibits a public official from participating
in a governmental decision in which he or she has a conflict of
interest.  Since public statements by Councilman Maxwell indicate
that he will most probably vote in favor of a modification of
Fortuna's general plan for this property (which approval is one
of a number of votes and permits needed before L & H can begin
construction), it is important for Councilman Maxwell and for
us to know that there is no question that his vote, for approval
or denial, is legal and not in violation of Government Code
§87100 or §4 of the Fortuna Conflict of Interest Code.

Neither L & H International, or any of its principals, has any
direct or indirect relationship with Councilman Maxwell,
financial, or otherwise, and to the best of our knowledge,
Councilman Maxwell stands to gain nothing by reason of this
development or the present sale of any portion of these 40 acres

It is our understanding that Government Code §87100 and §37103
will allow Councilman Maxwell to legally vote on this matter if
certain actions are taken prior to the Council meeting.

As stated in the FPPC's letter to Councilman Maxwell of
November 7, 1977, a copy of which we obtained from your office

Ted Prim, Esq.                                          November 14, 1977
Re: Opinion Request 77034                                         Page 2

Mr. John Braun is a "source of income" to Councilman Maxwell
within the past 12 months, and has two financial interests that
would in one in case, and could in the second case, be
"materially affected" by Councilman Maxwell's vote to modify
the general plan. But if Mr. Braun divests himself completely
from any interest he now has in the two parcels, would not duly
then remove from the arena any financial interests held by
Councilman Maxwell's "source of income", and thereby allow
Councilman Maxwell to legally vote?

There are at least four ways that a public official or a
"financial interest" can overcome an existing conflict of inter-
est for the public official by extinguishing the financial
interest in the "affected" property, or the interests in the
"source of income", as follows:

1) The official can sell his or her entire interest in
the "affected" property free and clear for loss than $250,
(Government Code §87103(c));

2) The official can exchange all his or her interest in
the "affected" property in consideration for dividends, interest
or premiums on a time or demand deposit in a financial
institution, shares in a credit union or insurance policy, or
any dividends, interest or any other return on a security which
is registered with the United States Securities and Exchange
Commission, thus recognizing no "income", as defined by the
Act, on the exchange (Government Code §82030(h)(7) and (8);

3) The official's "source of income" can completely divest
itself of all financial interest in the affected properties to
a person with whom the public official has no financial
relationship (Government Code §87100). "Completely" would
mean that the source of income is entirely "free and clear" and
retains no equitable interest, right to future consideration,
right of obligation to repurchase the property and no contin-
gency whatsoever tied to the outcome of any vote by the public
body; or

4) If the conflict exists because the public official is
a director, officer, trustee or non-salaried employee of the
financial interest, resignation from the position would extin-
guish the conflict of interest (Government Code §8713(d)).

In essence, these methods comply with Prop 9's conflicts of
interest standards by insulating a public official from using a
public position to affect his or her direct or indirect
financial interest. They are not "loopholes" in the law, but
rather examples of good law that recognizes the need to
prevent conflicts without covering those transactions where
there is little or no benefit to the public official.

Ted Prim, Esq.                                          November 14, 1977
Re: Opinion Request 77034                                         Page 2

We acknowledge that this case in Fortuna is not the usual one
where parties have already decided on a course of action and
now wish to determine whether such course will create a conflict
of interest. Here, the financial transactions contemplated
would not otherwise be pursued except for Government Code
§87100. Unfortunately the City Attorney's opinion Councilman
Maxwell that a conflict could be eliminated if Mr. Braun
completed his purchase of the property has already deprived
Councilman Maxwell of a source of income and has produced an
unexpected, undesirable capital gains tax. Also it is unfortunate
that L & H International must now consider the purchase of
unimproved land prior to receiving government approval for
development and further be required to purchase additional
land in Fortuna. And it is furthermore unfortunate that
Mr. Braun may be requested to forego his original plan to sell
the land after it has already been granted all necessary government approvals for development and if he sells that land in this
manner he required to take a large capital gains in a single
year. In summary, it is unfortunate that these actions taken
and contemplated are solely to comply with a statute which
presently defines as a conflict a vote from which Councilman
Maxwell has not or will not benefit at all.

It seems unlikely that many persons will be able to, or want to,
extinguish a conflict of interest through any of the first
three methods discussed above. But that should not in any way
tarnish them as legitimate and legal ways to comply with the
Act.

As I am sure you understand, it is important for all parties that
this matter be clarified immediately. We respectfully request
your assistance and appreciate your cooperation.

Very sincerely,

[signature]

JOHN H. HODGSON II

JHH:laj

sections.question: Whether Mr. Maxwell can vote on the proposed variance if, prior to the vote, Mr. Braun sells both parcels to L&H, the developer of the contemplated project.
sections.conclusion: Mr. Maxwell may vote on the proposed amendment to the General Plan if the sales agreement transferring the parcels in question from Mr. Braun to the developer does not reflect any of the three elements: (1) contract contingent on vote outcome; (2) Braun retaining equitable interest or future rights; or (3) sales price reflecting appreciation due to anticipated General Plan amendment. However, under the unique facts where Braun would not have sold without reliance on Maxwell's vote, the vote will have material financial effect if Braun receives appreciated value from L&H in anticipation of the General Plan change.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Councilman Maxwell legally vote on a proposed General Plan amendment affecting property previously owned by him if his current income source (Jack Braun) divests completely from the property before the vote?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Councilman Maxwell may vote on the General Plan amendment only if the sale agreement between Braun and the developer is entirely free of contingencies tied to the vote outcome, Braun retains no equitable interest or future rights, and the sales price does not reflect appreciation attributable to the anticipated zoning change. The FPPC concludes that under these specific circumstances, the sale would have a material financial effect on Maxwell's source of income, creating a disqualifying conflict.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (dated Nov 14, 1977 from Hodgson) and FPPC response letter (dated Nov 17, 1977 from Ted Prim). Extraction focuses on FPPC response. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Strong factual analysis regarding materiality of financial effects. Applicability of three-part test clearly articulated.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mr. Maxwell can vote on the proposed variance if, prior to the vote, Mr. Braun sells both parcels to L&H, the developer of the contemplated project.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Maxwell may vote on the proposed amendment to the General Plan if the sales agreement transferring the parcels in question from Mr. Braun to the developer does not reflect any of the three elements: (1) contract contingent on vote outcome; (2) Braun retaining equitable interest or future rights; or (3) sales price reflecting appreciation due to anticipated General Plan amendment. However, under the unique facts where Braun would not have sold without reliance on Maxwell's vote, the vote will have material financial effect if Braun receives appreciated value from L&H in anticipation of the General Plan change.

embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Ted Prim advises that Councilman Dudley Maxwell has a conflict of interest regarding a vote on a General Plan amendment affecting property owned by his income source Jack Braun, and can only vote if specific conditions regarding the sale to the developer are met.
